skill.name: micro-saas-launcher
skill.description: Expert in launching small, focused SaaS products fast - the indie hacker approach to building profitable software. Covers idea validation, MVP development, pricing, launch strategies, and growing to sustainable revenue. Ship in weeks, not months. Use when: micro saas, indie hacker, small saas, side project, saas mvp.
skill.summary: Plan, validate, price, and launch a focused micro-SaaS product.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found these are markdown or plain-language false positives. The skill is a business guidance document with no executable scripts, no network calls, no secret access, and no prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fences Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick matches are markdown code fences and inline skill names in a non-executable guidance file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file contains markdown examples and related skill names, not shell or Ruby execution. No executable command invocation or user-input command construction is present.

low_findings.title: Plain English Misclassified as Network Library Use
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network finding is triggered by the word requests in business advice, not by a Python HTTP library import or network call.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 187 is prose about saying no to feature requests. There is no Python code, URL, HTTP client, or data transmission behavior.

low_findings.title: Substring Matches Misclassified as Blocker Patterns
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance alerts are caused by ordinary product text, not cryptographic algorithms or system discovery commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are natural-language SaaS guidance and related skill names. No cryptographic API, weak cipher selection, or host reconnaissance command appears.

content.value_statement: Small software products often fail because founders build too much before validating demand. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code shape a lean launch plan from idea to early revenue.
actual_capabilities: Assess a micro-SaaS idea against buyer clarity, market pain, and distribution access.
Scope a two-week MVP around one core feature and a practical launch checklist.
Suggest simple pricing models based on customer value and alternative costs.
Identify launch risks such as weak distribution, unclear buyers, churn, and complex pricing.
Recommend practical solo-founder stack choices for auth, payments, email, hosting, and database needs.
limitations: Does not build the product or integrate third-party services by itself.
Does not replace legal, tax, accounting, or compliance advice.
Requires real customer research to validate demand and pricing.
Uses general startup patterns that must be adapted to each market.

use_cases.title: Validate a Niche SaaS Idea
use_cases.description: Turn a rough idea into customer questions, validation tests, and red flags before building.

use_cases.title: Plan a Two-Week MVP
use_cases.description: Define the smallest useful feature set, launch checklist, and service stack for a first release.

use_cases.title: Set Early Pricing
use_cases.description: Choose a simple pricing model and compare it against customer value, competitors, and manual alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Idea
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this micro-SaaS idea: [idea]. Identify the buyer, pain point, validation method, red flags, and next three actions.

prompt_templates.title: Scope My MVP
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a two-week MVP plan for [product]. Include the core feature, what to skip, setup tasks, launch tasks, and success metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Pricing
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a simple pricing strategy for [product]. Compare competitor pricing, manual work value, trial options, and the first paid plan.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Launch Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this micro-SaaS launch plan: [plan]. Find gaps in validation, distribution, onboarding, pricing, churn risk, and post-launch iteration.

output_examples.input: A founder wants to build a tool for accountants who manage client document requests.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify accountants as the buyer, document chasing as the pain, and pre-sales calls as the first validation step.

output_examples.input: A developer asks what to include in a first version of a small reporting SaaS.
A bootstrapped team has many feature requests before launch.
output_examples.output: The skill would separate launch-critical items from later improvements and recommend shipping the narrow workflow first.

best_practices: Start with a narrow buyer and one painful workflow.
Validate willingness to pay before building a full product.
Keep pricing, onboarding, and the first feature simple.
anti_patterns: Adding features before the core workflow is proven.
Pricing too low to support a sustainable business.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan micro-SaaS validation, MVP scope, pricing, launch steps, and early traction work.

faq.question: Can it build my SaaS application?
faq.answer: No. It provides launch guidance and planning. Product implementation requires separate coding work.

faq.question: Is it useful for non-technical founders?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains buyer validation, pricing, and launch choices in practical business terms.

faq.question: Can it choose a technology stack?
faq.answer: It can suggest common solo-founder tools, but final choices should match your team and product requirements.

faq.question: Does it guarantee revenue?
faq.answer: No. It improves planning and validation, but revenue depends on market demand, execution, and distribution.

faq.question: Should I use it before or after building?
faq.answer: Use it before building to reduce waste, then use it again to review pricing and launch readiness.
